items.correct: Energy used to maintain basic bodily functions at complete rest.
items.why: Students think BMR is about exercise. It's the baseline energy needed just to STAY ALIVE - breathing, heart, brain, organ function - typically 60-70% of total daily energy use even before any activity.
Students confuse fat- and water-soluble vitamins. Mnemonic: ADEK fat-soluble (stored in body fat - can build up to toxic levels). B and C water-soluble (excess excreted in urine).

items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.q: Henry BMR equation was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2005. “1985” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Mifflin-St Jeor BMR equation was published in:
items.why: It's 1990. “1980” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: For a 70 kg, 175 cm, 25-year-old man via Mifflin-St Jeor (10W+6.25H−5A+5), BMR kcal/d is:
items.why: Exact answer: 1674. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
